title: Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Act
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This law tells the Department of the Interior to create a program that helps replant trees and fix damaged land after natural disasters like wildfires or storms. The program will focus on federal lands and Native American lands that are too damaged to heal on their own. Each year, the department will make a list of the most important places that need help first. They can give money to groups or make agreements with companies to do the replanting and restoration work, and they can also help make sure there are enough seeds and baby trees available for these projects.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Program
sections.text:
The Secretary of the Interior must work with other government agencies to identify federal lands and Native American lands that need help growing back trees and plants after disasters. These are places where nature cannot recover on its own without help.

Within one year of this law passing and every year after that, the Secretary must make a list of areas that need this help. The Secretary will work with other agency leaders to create a list of the most important projects each year.

The Secretary can help these projects by giving grants (money awards), making contracts with organizations, or making agreements with groups to do the work. The Secretary can also help make sure there are enough seeds and young plants available for these projects.

The government agencies must reach out to many groups including Indian Tribes, states, territories, local governments, Alaska Native organizations, Native Hawaiian organizations, colleges and universities, other federal agencies that manage nearby land, and other interested groups.

Starting two years after this law passes and every year after that, the Secretary must send a report to Congress. The report must include a count of all lands that need help, a list of the most important projects and how they are going, information about grants and contracts given out, details about outreach efforts, and recommendations about gaps in seeds, plants, and funding that need to be addressed.

This program will end seven years after this law is passed.

This section defines important terms used in the law. A covered agency means the National Park Service, United States Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, Bureau of Reclamation, and Bureau of Indian Affairs. Covered lands means federal land managed by these agencies or Indian forest land and rangeland. Indian forest land or rangeland means land held in trust by the United States for Indian Tribes that has forests, grasslands, brush, or similar plants, or used to have this vegetation and can be restored. Indian Tribe means any recognized Native American or Alaska Native tribe, band, nation, pueblo, village, or community on the official federal list. Natural regeneration means when nature can restore itself without human help. Reforestation means replanting trees in areas that used to have forests. Restoration means helping damaged ecosystems recover by bringing back the right mix of plants and natural community structure. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. Unplanned disturbance means unexpected events that damage ecosystems like wildfires, insect outbreaks, diseases, or severe weather.
